H: The lightest and the most abundant element in the universe. Forms the source of acidity, H<sub>3</sub>O<sup>+</sup> (solvented H<sup>+</sup>). Non-metallic hydrides except for H<sub>2</sub>O are mostly toxic or harmful. Metal ionic hydride are good reducing agents in organic settings, like LiAlH<sub>4</sub>; covalent ones are good energy souces, like LaNi<sub>5</sub>H<sub>6</sub>. Boasts three common isotopes, non-radioactive Protium (the normal one), Deuterium (used for biological tracing) and radioactive Tritium (for fluorescence).

He: This least reactive and hardest to liquify element has the highest ionization energy. Substitutes H<sub>2</sub> as a safer lightweighed gas in ballons or airships. Becomes Superfluid near absolute zero, with no viscosity and 800 times the heat conductivity of copper.

Li: The metal with the lowest density and largest reduction potential. Reacts with water calmly and floats on it. Has intensly red emitting wavelength. Forms one of the most akaline non-gaseous material, tert-butyl Lithium, which is know for self conbustion in air. Commonly found in high-perfomance batteries especially in cars and as a medicine for Bipolar.

Be: A sweet-tasting and potently toxic light element, its dust may cause permanent lung disease (Berylliosis). Its properties resemble that of Aluminum, being amphoteric and forming relatively covalent compounds for an alkali-earth. Its used in X-ray windows, highend headphone diaphragms, and aerospace materials, like the mirror of James-Webb telescope. It's name comes from the precious mineral beryl, including emerald and aquamarine.

B: Named after Borax for welding and melting metals. Has a Fluorescent-ish Green flame. Forms complex metal compounds such as CaB<sub>6</sub> and pure substances like B<sub>12</sub>, both of the examples has spherical boron structure. Boron Hydrides are extremely toxic and flamable, often including 'bridge bonding' and used to be considered the rocket fuel of the future for its energy-density. Li, Be and B can't be formed inside stars in large amounts because of low nuclear binding energy, they're mostly created by cosmic ray.

C: The building block of life and all organic things. Forms organic compounds like Alkanes, Alkenes, Alkynes, Aromatic Hydrocarbons, Alcohols, Phenols, Ethers, Aldehydes, Ketones, Carboxylic Acids, Esters and so on.

N: Makes up 78% of the air but is relatively inert that almost no organism except for certain bacteria can use N<sub>2</sub> directly. Alkali-earth metals as well as Lithium can break the triple bond of N<sub>2</sub> at r.t.. Notably, lightening is referred to as the wife of rice plants ('Inazuma') in Japanese for producing Nitric compounds that helps plants thrive. Liquid nitrogen is a cheap cryogen, good for shattering flowers, rubber balls and even making room-temperature superconductors behave less mysteriously. Nitrogen compunds are behind many explosives like TNT, RDX, and PETN, or even more extreme ones like C<sub>2</sub>N<sub>14</sub>, or NaN<sub>3</sub> which was used in car airbags, and hydrazine compounds which was used as rocket fuel. The most sensative inorganic N compound is NI<sub>3</sub>, detonating in contact with a gust of wind or left alone for some time

O: The element that makes fire and rust possible in Earth's atmosphere. O<sub>2</sub> is essential for aerobic life, but killed nearly all anaerobic lifeforms in The Great Oxidation Event caused by Cyanobacteria around 2.4 billion years ago. O<sub>3</sub> shields us from solar UV yet is toxic and highly oxidative and can be used as disinfectant of water. Superoxide anions causes oxidative stress in animals, while Peroxide structures appears in plants, for example as Artemisin (anti-malaria medicine). In liquid form, oxygen gas is pale blue and paramagnetic, while ozone appears ultramarine-blue, and concentrated H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> is also light blue and can quietly dissolve many metals that acids ignore.

F: The most reactive and oxydative nonmetal; even glass, oxygen and noble gases like Kr and Xe can be oxydized in its presence. F<sub>2</sub> has a strange pale yellow color and can make organic materials, sulfur, iodine and many metals burst into flame on contact. Many died trying to purify F<sub>2</sub> because its compound HF destroys human lungs and bones, before Henri Moissan finally did it. Most of its chemistry hides in seemingly tame things like Teflon pans, fluorinated anesthetics and the fluoride in toothpaste. The name, fluorine, is misleading since fluorite is sometimes fluorescent due to trace lathanides, but fluorine itself is not.

Ne: A noble gas famous more for its glow than its chemistry. True neon signs emit an intense red-orange color; most other sign colors are actually from Ar or Hg vapor. Liquid neon is an efficient refrigerant, but too rare and expensive to compete with cheaper cryogens. Its name, Neon, stands for 'new'. Some unstable Neon ions have been found under spectrometry, including Ne+, (NeAr)+, NeH+ and (HeNe)+.

Na: Found in table salt, soda, baking soda, hypo, lye and many other daily necessities, but as a metal it is a soft even squishy, shiny solid that can be cut with a knife. Its intense yellow D-line dominates street lamps and is used as a calibration line in spectroscopy. Molten Na is a heat-transfer fluid in some fast breeder reactors and experimental Stirling engines. Na reacts with water vigorously and upon burning, forms its peroxide. All Alkali metals dissloves in liquid ammonia forming a deep blue to metallic color depending on concentration of the metal, which comes from solvated electron. Similar phenomenon can also appear in their reaction with water with high speed camera, or if dissolved in liquid KCl, forming a red electron salt.

Mg: Burns with a blinding white flame that used to light early photography and flares. Mg<sup>2+</sup> sits at the heart of chlorophyll, so every green leaf is a quiet magnesium factory. Metallic magnesium is so light that it can float on strong salt solutions and is used in sacrificial anodes to protect ships and pipelines.

Al: The most abundant metal in Earth’s crust but once more precious than silver, so early emperors had aluminum cutlery. Its thin oxide film makes it unexpectedly corrosion-resistant, enabling soda cans, airplanes and mirrors. Freshly formed Al powder is so reactive it can rip oxygen from rust in thermite reactions and weld rails together.

Si: The backbone of both sand and silicon chips. In nature it prefers to be locked in SiO<sub>2</sub> frameworks, yet in labs it forms long chains and cage-like molecules that mimic organic chemistry. Pure silicon glows with orange light when heated and its carefully doped wafers quietly run most of modern civilization.

P: Exists in dramatically different forms: white phosphorus glows faintly in moist air and can self-ignite, while red phosphorus is much calmer and hides on matchbox striking surfaces. In biology, P runs the cell’s energy currency ATP and builds DNA backbones as PO<sub>4</sub><sup>3−</sup> groups.

S: Recognizable by the faint smell from volcanoes and matches, though the worst-smelling sulfur compounds are actually organosulfur molecules. Sulfur chains can be stretched like rubber when molten and snap back into brittle rings when cooled. In batteries, polysulfides quietly shuttle electrons in some of the highest energy-density chemistries.

Cl: The greenish-yellow gas that gave us both chlorine bleach and chemical warfare in WWI. In ionic form Cl<sup>−</sup> is so friendly that it balances charges in our blood and seawater. Organic chlorides store information in PVC cards, make plastics flame-retardant and sometimes turn simple molecules into stubborn pollutants.

Ar: The third most common gas in air but chemically almost invisible. It fills incandescent bulbs and protects welds from oxygen. Liquid argon glows faintly under ionizing radiation and is used in massive underground detectors to listen for elusive neutrinos and dark matter candidates.

K: The most abundant cation inside animal and plant cells controlling the relaxation of muscle cells in animals. It was named after the K<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>-rich  ash of plants, potash. K is more reactive and less dense then Na, always explodes in contact with water. Forms even superoxides in air or upon burning with a pink-violet flame, which was used for re-breathing material in early planes and submarines. Unlike Na, it cannot be stored under mineral oil for prolonged time. K is the only alkali metal that turns blue when tarnished and evaporates intensely green vapor, condensing as a purple layer. K can form a orange colored stable ozonide KO<sub>3</sub>. Its alloy with Na, Nak is liquid at r.t. and was used as colling agent in LMRs.

Ca: Responsible for both hard rocks and soft bones. In water, Ca<sup>2+</sup> builds up scale in kettles and makes soap scum, yet in cells it acts as a precise signaling ion that triggers muscle contraction and neurotransmitter release. Quicklime CaO once fueled self-heating cans and field rations by slaking with water.

Sc: A shy transition metal that prefers to hide in rare minerals. Its trivalent ion behaves like an extra-light lanthanide, and trace additions of Sc can significantly strengthen aluminum alloys used in aerospace. Scandium iodide is used in some metal-halide lamps to mimic sunlight for stadiums.

Ti: Strong as some steels but much lighter, and it keeps its cool by forming a stubborn TiO<sub>2</sub> skin. Fresh titanium burns with white sparks, yet the same oxide becomes a harmless whitening pigment in toothpaste and sunscreen. Ti also decorates jewelry with vivid interference colors produced simply by controlled anodizing.

V: A trace metal in steel that quietly raises toughness and temperature resistance. Vanadium oxides can switch between metallic and insulating states near room temperature, making window coatings that change transparency with heat. In biology, vanadium-dependent haloperoxidases in seaweed help release halogenated compounds into ocean air.

Cr: Best known for chrome plating that resists rust and gives mirrors their shine. Cr<sup>3+</sup> compounds color rubies and emeralds, while Cr<sup>6+</sup> ones are powerful oxidants and notorious carcinogens. Stainless steel owes much of its corrosion resistance to a thin layer of chromium oxide that constantly heals itself.

Mn: Manganese glassmakers once used this element to decolorize greenish glass, earning it the name glassmaker's soap. In biology Mn-containing enzymes detoxify reactive oxygen species, and in batteries MnO<sub>2</sub> quietly cathodes most disposable alkaline cells. Concentrated permanganate turns into a deep purple oxidizer that can ignite organics on drying.

Fe: Earth’s core is largely molten iron alloy, spinning our magnetic field. On the surface Fe cycles between Fe<sup>2+</sup> and Fe<sup>3+</sup>, giving us rust, blood’s red color in hemoglobin and many industrial catalysts. Meteorites rich in nickel-iron were among the first metals shaped by humans long before smelting was invented.

Co: Gives cobalt blue glass and pigments their characteristic deep color. Vitamin B<sub>12</sub> uniquely centers on a cobalt ion, making Co an essential but trace nutrient. Cobalt-based magnets (Alnico, SmCo) retain magnetization even at high temperatures, useful in motors and spacecraft.

Ni: Originally found in ores that fooled miners, nickeline was said to be copper ore bewitched by mountain spirits. Nickel resists corrosion in many environments, so it plates coins and guitar strings. Some microorganisms even use Ni-containing enzymes to process hydrogen gas as an energy source.

Cu: One of the first metals shaped by humans, forming tools long before the Bronze Age. Metallic copper is an excellent conductor and antimicrobial surface; door handles slowly kill bacteria without disinfectant. Blue copper proteins shuttle single electrons in biology with very characteristic intense absorption bands.

Zn: Used in brass for millennia, but isolated as a pure metal only relatively late. Zn coating (galvanizing) sacrifices itself to protect steel beams and rooftops. In the body, Zn-finger proteins read DNA sequences, making this humble metal a key player in gene regulation.

Ga: Melts just above room temperature, so a Ga spoon will slowly liquify in a warm hand. Its low toxicity and high boiling point make it a curious material for high-temperature thermometers. GaAs and GaN are behind many high-power microwave devices and blue LEDs, shrinking radar sets and lighting entire cities. Gallium, unlike mercury soaks glassware because it forms an oxide layer in air. Similar to Mercury, Gallium is also banned from commercial airlines due to its strong capability of alloying with alluminum.

Ge: The semimetal behind the first ever semiconductor element. Can be purified to over 9N with GeH<sub>4</sub> CVD, the latter being found in Jupiter's atmosphere. Ge is the most transparent metal under infrared light and is used for nighvis and other optical lenses.

As: Historically known as both a pigment and a poison; Scheele's green and Paris green wallpapers slowly released toxic As compounds into Victorian homes, and famoously killed Napolean in his house on St. Helena Island. In electronics, GaAs and InAs alloys quietly use arsenic to carry high-speed signals in microwave chips and infrared LEDs. Arsenic is also, counterintuitively, a necessary element for life, and toxic arsenic trioxide can cure leukemia in a micro dosage.

Se: Discovered as an impurity in sulfuric acid plants, selenium later became famous for photocells that convert light directly into electricity. Selenium rectifiers powered some of the earliest electronics. In biology, Se-containing enzymes such as glutathione peroxidase help mop up peroxides that would otherwise damage cells.

Br: One of the two elements that are liquid at room temperature, with a dark red-brown color, red vapor and a suffocating smell. Brominated compounds once dominated flame retardants and some sedatives, leaving traces of Br in dust and sediments worldwide. Silver bromide grains in classic photographic film captured light as latent images. Hyperbromic acid was the most unstable and oxidative +7 halogen acids that theoretically exists, first prepared with fission of H<sub>2</sub>SeO<sub>4</sub>.

Kr: A noble gas that mostly minds its own business, but when excited in discharge tubes it emits sharp spectral lines used to define length standards for a time. Krypton difluoride is one of the most electronegative and oxidative compounds, even oxidating oxygen and other noble gases under extreme conditions.

Rb: Slightly heavier cousin of K, so reactive that it can shatter glass ampoules on freezing due to expansion. Rubidium atomic clocks exploit a narrow microwave transition in Rb atoms to keep time with astonishing precision in GPS satellites and telecom networks.

Sr: Known for its brilliant crimson emission in fireworks and signal flares. In bones, radioactive <sup>90</sup>Sr can replace Ca and remain for decades, making fallout particularly dangerous. Strontium titanate crystals were once sold as artificial diamonds due to their high refractive index and dispersion.

Y: Often hides among lanthanides in ores, and Y<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>:Eu phosphors made color TV screens glow red in the 20th century. Modern white LEDs still rely on YAG:Ce phosphors to convert blue light to warm white. YBCO, a yttrium-barium-copper oxide, is one of the best-known high-T<sub>c</sub> superconductors.

Zr: Zirconium metal resists corrosion so well that it lines the cladding of nuclear fuel rods, holding back fission products while letting neutrons through. The gemstone zircon owes its high sparkle to dense ZrSiO<sub>4</sub>, and cubic zirconia simulates diamonds in jewelry at a fraction of the cost.

Nb: Added in tiny amounts, niobium stabilizes steels in gas pipelines and skyscrapers. Superconducting Nb-Ti and Nb<sub>3</sub>Sn wires generate the strong magnetic fields in MRI scanners and particle accelerators. Historically it was confused with tantalum for decades because their oxides are so similar.

Mo: Molybdenum steel was key to early high-speed tool steels and tank armor. In enzymes like nitrogenase, Mo helps bacteria break the triple bond of N<sub>2</sub>, quietly feeding the global nitrogen cycle. Molybdate glasses can change color reversibly under voltage as electrochromic windows.

Tc: The lightest element with no stable isotopes, discovered not in rocks but in cyclotron targets and later in red giant stars. Technetium-99m is a workhorse medical radionuclide, tracing blood flow and organ function with short-lived gamma emissions that minimize dose.

Ru: A rare platinum-group metal that can harden Pt to withstand hot acid mixtures. Ruthenium red is a vivid dye that stains biological membranes. Ru-based complexes have also been explored as light-absorbing dyes in some of the earliest dye-sensitized solar cells.

Rh: Extremely reflective and corrosion-resistant, rhodium is used to flash-coat mirrors and jewelry, giving that bright white finish. In car exhaust systems, Rh catalysts help strip NO<sub>x</sub> of oxygen, turning pollutants back into N<sub>2</sub> and O<sub>2</sub>. Its rarity makes it one of the most expensive precious metals.

Pd: The most Hydrogen-phillic metal, absorbing 900 times in volumn of H<sub>2</sub>. Has the weirdest base electron configuration with a completely empty outer shell. And is the most reactive of the Platinum group, reacting even with nitric acid at r.t..

Ag: Long before antibiotics, silver coins in water jugs kept microbial growth down. Dilute silver solutions still find use in wound dressings and eye drops. In photography, tiny Ag grains recorded images, and in electronics silver-filled pastes print entire circuits on ceramics and flexible films.

Cd: The toxic element that appeared in warm-color paints for many artworks, famously Claude Monet's Haystacks. Both red CdSe and yellow CdS are strong semiconductive pigments, never banned completely due to their strong hiding power. Cadmium concentrates in rice plants, causing the notorious Itai-itai disease along the banks of  Jinzu river in 20th century Japan. Cadmium targets mostly the bone and the kidney. It is used as anti-corrosive layer of airplane bolts and was once used in Cd-Ni batteries in toys.

In: Named after the indigo line in its spectrum, indium squeaks when bent and can wet glass and metals like water. Indium tin oxide (ITO) is the transparent conductor that makes touch screens and LCDs respond to your fingers.

Sn: Tin's gentler image contrasts with its complex history: the Bronze Age depended on mixing Sn with Cu, yet pure tin can slowly crumble and turn into a non-metallic 'diamond structure' under 13.2°C called tin pest, famously causing the winter coats of Napolean's army to freeze in Russia. Tinplate cans rely on only a few micrometers of Sn to keep steel from rusting while food sits safely inside. Tin is often considered safe, but its organic compounds, especially tributyltin, are highly toxic.

Sb: Stibnite, Sb<sub>2</sub>S<sub>3</sub>, forms long metallic crystals that ancient cultures ground into eye cosmetics. Small amounts of Sb in lead alloys harden bullets and type metal. In modern times, antimony compounds help plastics resist catching fire but raise questions about long-term toxicity. Sb also famously forms the most acidic lewis acid HSbF<sub>6</sub>.

Te: Tellurium compounds often smell strongly of garlic, a side effect even noticed on the breath of exposed chemists. Adding a pinch of Te to steel improves machinability, and bismuth telluride is a classic thermoelectric, turning heat gradients directly into electricity. CdTe is a high performance photoelectric material for solar panels.

I: The heaviest nonmetal that is solid at room temperature, forming violet crystals that sublime easily. Iodine sublimes into a deep purple vapor, and in alcohol it's yellow,  staining skin as an antiseptic. In the thyroid, I<sup>−</sup> becomes part of hormones that control metabolism, hence the need to iodize table salt for inland areas. Since Iodine concentrates in sea plants like seaweed, so seaside residents may no need iodized salt. Its oxide I<sub>2</sub>O<sub>5</sub> is used to detect CO since it oxides it at r.t., while I<sub>2</sub> itself can form polycations in Oleum such as I<sub>5</sub><sup>+</sup> which is dark green, and polyanions in iodide solution such as I<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup> which is orange.

Xe: A noble gas with a surprisingly rich chemistry, forming bright xenon fluorides under the right conditions. Xenon lamps produce intense white light for projectors and surgical lighting. In anesthesia, xenon gas can act as a high-end but extremely expensive anesthetic with rapid recovery.

Cs: The softest and most reactive metal; fresh cesium is uniquely golden, resembling the color of Pyrite (Fool's gold). It can be dented with a fingernail and melts slightly above room temperature. In air, cesium melts from its oxidation heat and self conbusts with a pink flame. Its hyperfine transition in Cs-133 defines the second in the SI system, making cesium atomic clocks the heartbeat of GPS and internet time. Cesium is so reactive, it even forms near-ionic compound with other metals, like CsAu or CsCu, wherethe other metal is in -1 oxidation state.

Ba: Emits a characteristic apple-green color in fireworks. Insoluble barium sulfate paints the digestive tract for X-ray imaging as a contrast agent, while soluble Ba salts are dangerously toxic. In old vacuum tubes inside electric circuits, Ba is that pale golden mirror flashed from getters to mop up residual gases and keep the vacuum clean.

Hf: Chemically twins zirconium so closely that separating the two took decades. Hf has an enormous neutron absorption cross section, making it ideal for control rods in nuclear reactors, while Zr with low absorption becomes the fuel cladding, an unusual division of labor for chemical siblings.

Ta: Tantalum carries charge silently in tiny electrolytic capacitors that stabilize power rails in phones and laptops. It forms one of the most corrosion-resistant oxides known, allowing Ta crucibles to hold aggressive molten salts and metals that would attack almost anything else such as aquaregia and oleum. However, Ta easily dissolves in alkali such as molten NaOH as well as fluoride-environments like fluo-aquaregia.

W: Tungsten has the highest melting point of any pure metal, so hot filaments in old incandescent bulbs could glow white without vaporizing immediately. Tungsten carbide tips in drills and saw blades cut through steel and rock. Some organisms even use tungsten in rare enzymes that operate at extreme temperatures.

Re: Extremely rare in the crust, rhenium sneaks into molybdenite concentrates and is recovered from smelter fumes. Its high-temperature superalloys and turbine blade coatings survive inside jet engines where few materials remain solid. Rhenium filaments once served in mass spectrometers and ion gauges.

Os: The densest naturally occurring element with a unique greyish blue tint, forming very hard, brittle metallic crystals. Its most important compound OsO<sub>4</sub> is slowly formed when Osmium dust is exposed to air, letting out a distinct chlorine-like foul smell, giving the metal its name Osmos ('smell'). OsO<sub>4</sub> is volatile, powerfully oxidizing and used to stain biological membranes for electron microscopy and for catalyzing ‘Sharpless asymmetric dihydroxylation’, though it must be handled with great care due to toxicity and volatility. It was said to be used in a terrorist attack in the UK.

Ir: Traces of iridium in global clay layers mark the asteroid impact that ended the dinosaurs. Iridium crucibles endure high temperatures and corrosive melts in crystal growth. Its alloys with Pt and Rh serve as long-lasting thermocouple tips and fountain pen nibs. It is the only element that is proved to have a +9 oxidation state.

Pt: Platinum resists corrosion so well that ancient Egyptian jewelry still shines after millennia. It quietly catalyzes reactions in car exhausts and chemical plants, shuffling hydrogen and oxygen between molecules. Dissolving Pt in aqua regia once required two acids and produced volatile complexes that even fooled thieves during wartime. PtF<sub>6</sub> is a powerful oxidizing agent capable of oxidizing oxygen and other noble gases, forms both the first ever discovered noble gas compound XePtF<sub>6</sub> and oxygen compound O<sub>2</sub>PtF<sub>6</sub>.

Au: Gold is one of the few metals with a distinct color, thanks to relativistic effects shifting its absorption into blue. In nanoscale form, gold colloids display red or purple colors and underpin some rapid diagnostic tests. Thin gold leaf can be beaten so thin that light passes through with a greenish tint.

Hg: Mercury is a liquid metal at room temperature, once used in thermometers and barometers, and even as a reflective pool in some early telescopes. Organomercury compounds are among the most bioaccumulative toxins, highlighted by Minamata disease. Yet in fluorescent lamps, a trace of Hg vapor is what turns electricity into ultraviolet light for phosphors to convert.

Tl: Thallium salts once appeared in rat poisons and even some home remedies before their severe toxicity was fully recognized; hair loss is an early symptom of poisoning. Tl-based superconductors briefly held high-T<sub>c</sub> records but lost favor due to handling hazards.

Pb: Lead pipes carried water in ancient Rome and the word plumbing still remembers it. Pb gives glass a high refractive index and pleasing weight in crystalware, but airborne lead from gasoline left a measurable layer in polar ice. Lead-acid batteries remain one of the oldest rechargeable technologies still in heavy use.

Bi: Oddly for a heavy element, bismuth has very low toxicity and even appears in some stomach medicines due to its salts undergoing intense hydrolysis even in acidic solutions like that of the stomach, forming protective layers of insoluble bismuth oxycompounds. Molten Bi expands on freezing like water, making bismuth alloys useful in low-melting safety plugs that open when systems overheat. Its iridescent hopper crystals owe their colors to thin oxide films, not impurities.

La: Lanthanum lends its name to the lanthanide series and improves glass for camera lenses by raising refractive index without adding much color. LaNi<sub>5</sub>H<sub>6</sub> and related hydrides store hydrogen reversibly, making them candidates for metal-hydride batteries and heat pumps.

Ce: Cerium is abundant enough that its oxide coats self-cleaning oven liners, assisting in burning off food residues. In lighter flints, mischmetal rich in Ce showers sparks when scraped. Ceria in car exhaust catalysts toggles between Ce<sup>3+</sup> and Ce<sup>4+</sup>, storing and releasing oxygen atoms as needed.

Pr: Praseodymium gives a distinctive green color to some glasses and ceramics. Mixed with Nd, it helps make powerful permanent magnets found in wind turbines and headphones. A special Pr-doped glass, didymium, filters the yellow sodium line, protecting glassblowers' eyes from glare.

Nd: Neodymium magnets are behind the compact motors in hard drives, drones and electric vehicles. Nd-doped YAG lasers emit intense infrared beams used for cutting, welding and medical procedures. Despite the name new twin, Nd was hiding together with Pr in the same ore for a long time before separation.

Pm: Promethium has no stable isotopes and is extremely rare in nature, mainly produced in nuclear reactors or found in trace amounts in uranium ores. Its beta emissions have powered small luminous paints and experimental atomic batteries for remote devices where changing cells is impossible.

Sm: Samarium-cobalt magnets were an important step before Nd-Fe-B, combining high coercivity with good temperature stability. Samarium oxide appears in special ceramics and optical glasses. In nuclear reactors, Sm-149 is a strong neutron absorber that slowly builds up as spent fuel ages, quietly changing reactivity.

Eu: The only lanthanide that reacts with cold water vigorously and forms the most stable 2+ lanthanide compounds. Eu<sub>2+</sub> and Eu<sub>3+</sub> are used for green and red light for RGB in some screens, and for UV anti-counterfeit ink on bank notes. Eu<sub>2+</sub> salts are transparent while Eu<sub>3+</sub> ones are mildly pink. In air, Eu tarnishes quickly, turing drak green(Eu<sub>2</sub>(CO<sub>3</sub>)<sub>3</sub> and Eu<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>) with red dots (EuO).

Gd: Gadolinium has seven unpaired electrons, making it strongly paramagnetic; this property is exploited in MRI contrast agents that brighten blood vessels and tumors. Gd-based compounds also appear in neutron shields, as Gd nuclei readily capture slow neutrons.

Tb: Terbium-doped phosphors produce bright green light in CRTs and some fluorescent lamps. Tb can also tune the magnetostriction of alloys like Terfenol-D, which expands and contracts in magnetic fields and serves in precise actuators and sonar transducers.

Dy: Dysprosium, literally hard to get, stabilizes magnets at high temperatures, especially in hybrid car motors where Nd-Fe-B magnets would otherwise demagnetize. Dy-doped glass filters strongly in the infrared and can protect sensors or eyes from laser light.

Ho: Holmium has one of the highest magnetic moments of any element; a few atoms on a surface can store a classical bit in some low-temperature experiments. Holmium oxides add yellow and brown tints to glass and ceramics used in art and industry.

Er: Erbium-doped silica fibers form the heart of modern long-distance communication, amplifying 1.55 μm signals directly in the cable with light. Under the right excitation, Er<sup>3+</sup> ions emit a distinctive pinkish glow in glasses and crystals.

Tm: Thulium is scarce but useful; Tm-doped lasers emit in the eye-safer infrared range useful for surgery and LIDAR. Radioactive Tm-170 once served in portable X-ray sources for field inspection of welds and pipes.

Yb: Ytterbium appears in some of the most stable optical lattice clocks, where cold Yb atoms tick away time more regularly than any mechanical device. Yb-doped fiber lasers produce high-power beams used for cutting metals with remarkable efficiency.

Lu: Lutetium sits at the end of the lanthanide series with the smallest ionic radius. Lutetium tantalate crystals are among the densest scintillators for X-ray detection. The isotope Lu-177 has become important in targeted cancer therapies, delivering radiation directly to tumor cells.
